10 of 10 sections live
lpo.plus
Induction Pack
Prepared for
Mel Macdonald — Stanhope Gardens LPO

This is a short, practical guide for the staff who'll use localmile.plus at the counter. It's designed to be read in twenty minutes, returned to whenever something looks unfamiliar, and never to feel like homework.

Move through it section by section using the sidebar, or jump straight to whatever you need. Every screenshot opens to full size when you click it.

Version 1.5.1 — Full pack
Sections All 10 sections live for review
Section 01

What localmile.plus is for:

It exists for one reason: to ensure a Licensed Post Office can confidently capture a pickup request, whether received by phone, email, or at the counter, and trust that the system will capture all the required information.

That sentence sounds simple. It isn't. Most software written for back-office work ends up creating more work than it removes. localmile.plus is deliberately built the other way around. It does one job, and it tries to do it without asking you to learn anything you don't already know.

✓ What it does

  • Books a parcel pickup or lodgement in under a minute.
  • Holds the initial job until the customer accepts our standard T&Cs, then passes it to the franchisee driver automatically (only when they are paying for the service, not required when LPO pays).
  • Shows every job for the day at a glance — open, done, and waiting.
  • Manages recurring customers without re-entering them every week.
  • Sends the customer a clean confirmation so they don't ring back.

✕ What it doesn't

  • It doesn't replace the MailPlus NetSuite billing system. Invoicing for LPO and self-funded new customer accounts still happens there.
  • It doesn't take customer payments at the counter.
  • It doesn't need 40-page manuals. If a button confuses you, that's our bug, not your blind spot.
The promise
localmile.plus should retire your whiteboard.
If at any point you find yourself reaching for a pen and a sticky note to make sure a job doesn't get missed, please tell us. That is feedback we badly want to hear.
01 / 10
Section 02

Your dashboard, at a glance.

When you log in to localmile.plus you land on the Job Manager. Everything you need during a normal day is reachable from this single screen.

⌕ Click to enlarge
The Job Manager — your home base. Everything else is one click away from the left sidebar.

The left sidebar, top to bottom

The sidebar groups your tools into two sections: Logistics Management (the things you do daily) and Administration (the things you do occasionally).

Logistics Management

Job Manager
Your home base. Today's jobs, pending requests, completed jobs, history. Where you'll spend most of your time.
Awaiting T&C
Customers who are paying for the service but haven't yet accepted the Terms & Conditions. They disappear from this list automatically once they accept.
Recurring Schedules
Calendar view of every standing pickup arrangement. Skip a visit, change collection days, or end a series. Note — services are for business weekday only with your State's Public Holidays automatically excluded.

Administration

Share Booking Link
Generates a link a customer can use to view their booking history. Optional.
Customer Hub
Every customer your LPO has ever booked. One click to start a new job for any of them.
Invoices
Read-only invoice history. The numbers come from NetSuite.
Operational Insights
Light reporting. Useful monthly; ignore daily.
Service Area
The geographic area you can offer services to automatically. You will rarely touch this. If you need to service a customer outside this area, contact MailPlus.
My Profile
Your details and password. Set and forget.

The Customer Hub at a glance

If a customer rings and you recognise the name, the fastest path to a new job is the Customer Hub. Every existing customer is here as a card with everything you need on one face.

⌕ Click to enlarge
The Customer Hub. Notice the status badges — they tell you whether a customer is ready to book.
Reading the status badges
Active Awaiting T&C

Active — the customer has accepted T&Cs and any new job will flow through automatically.

Awaiting T&C — the customer was booked but hasn't yet clicked the link in the email we sent them. The job is paused until they do.

02 / 10
Section 03

Booking your first job for a new customer.

A business customer rings, emails or visits the post office and asks if you can collect parcels for them. The next ninety seconds are this section.

What you need from the caller

  • Their company name.
  • A contact first and last name, an email address, and a phone number.
  • Their pickup address — full street, suburb, postcode.
  • Whether they want a one-off pickup or a regular arrangement (and on which days).
  • Anything unusual about the pickup — “ring buzzer 3,” “parcels in the back office,” that kind of thing.

From Job Manager, click BOOK NEW JOB. (For an existing customer, go via Customer Hub and use NEW JOB FOR CUSTOMER or find their card and select the + symbol — that skips Step 1 entirely.) The booking flow has three steps:

1
Step
Site information
2
Step
Service & schedule
3
Step
Review

Step 1 — Site information

⌕ Click to enlarge
Step 1 captures who the customer is and where the pickup happens.

Fill in the fields top to bottom. Three things that aren't obvious from the screen:

  • Start typing the address and let it auto-complete from Google. This populates suburb, state, and postcode for you. Only edit those three fields manually if the address bar can't find the place.
  • Special Instructions is optional — but if there's anything the driver needs to know (gate code, buzzer number, ask-for-Sue), put it here. The franchisee will see it on every job.
  • Click VERIFY ADDRESS at the bottom. If the address can't be confirmed, the system will tell you here rather than letting you book a job the driver can't find.

Step 2 — Service & schedule

⌕ Click to enlarge
Choose who pays, which direction the parcels go, when, and whether it's a one-off or recurring.

This is the busiest screen but it breaks down into four decisions:

  1. Billing Option — Customer Pays or LPO Pays. Most business customers will be Customer Pays. LPO Pays is typically used when your post office is absorbing the cost to protect usage from a competitor.
  2. Pickup & Delivery Type — Site → LPO is the standard parcel collection. LPO → Site is for delivering items to that customer (like PO box mail, stationery). Round Trip is both.
  3. Booking Date — The date the job should happen. Defaults to the next business day.
  4. Time Constraints — Leave this blank in almost every case.
⚠ About the time constraint field

Set a time only if the customer has a genuine constraint — for example, parcels are not ready before 2:00 pm so the driver shouldn't arrive earlier.

Don't set a time just because the customer mentioned one. The franchisee works pickups into their normal route, and a time you don't actually need creates back-and-forth that delays the whole process.

At the bottom, the question “Is this a recurring job?” is the fork in the road:

  • If they want a one-off pickup — leave it on No and click NEXT.
  • If they want a regular arrangement — click Yes and select the weekdays they want to be collected on. (Mon–Fri only; weekends aren't supported.)

Step 3 — Review

⌕ Click to enlarge
A final summary. Read it back to the customer on the phone before you click REQUEST JOB.

This is your last chance to catch a typo. Read the summary aloud. The four lines that matter most:

  • Customer name — make sure it's spelled the way they want it on their invoice.
  • Type — Scheduled (recurring) or Job Booking (one-off). If wrong, click Modify Selection and go back.
  • Frequency — for recurring, the list of weekdays. For one-off, a single date.
  • Total Price — make sure this matches what you quoted on the phone.

If everything is right, click REQUEST JOB.

What happens next

Once you click REQUEST JOB, localmile.plus does three things in the background — you'll see them on a loading screen:

  1. It registers the new customer in the billing and dispatch systems.
  2. It notifies all parties and also sends the customer an email asking them to accept our standard Terms & Conditions if they are paying for the service.
  3. It moves the job into a holding pattern — the job won't go to the driver until the franchisee and LPO have agreed on the service. For customer-pays jobs, the terms & conditions (T&Cs) must also be accepted before then.

This is normal. Tell the customer on the phone:

📞 What to say to the customer at the end of the call

“You'll get an email from MailPlus in the next couple of minutes asking you to confirm your details. Just tap the green button at the bottom and tick the box. Once you've done that, we're good to go for [Tuesday-adjust the day to match the booking]. If you don't see the email, have a quick check in your spam folder.”

The email comes from kerry.oneill@mailplus.com.au — not from your post office.

Section 4 walks through what the customer sees on their side. Section 5 covers the franchisee coordination step that follows.

03 / 10
Section 04

The T&C gate.

For customer-pays jobs, there is one short pause between you clicking REQUEST JOB and the franchisee getting the work. The customer has to accept our standard terms. This section is what they see — and what you do while you wait.

When the gate applies — and when it doesn't

Customer Pays — yes. The customer must accept T&Cs before the job moves to the franchisee. This is the most common case.

LPO Pays — no. The job goes straight to coordination. There is no T&C step because the customer is not entering a billing relationship with MailPlus.

What the customer receives

Within a minute or two of you submitting the booking, the customer gets an email from kerry.oneill@mailplus.com.au. The subject line begins “Almost there — confirm your pickup service details.” It comes from MailPlus head office, not from your post office, and not from the bookings@localmile.plus mailbox. Two reasons that matters:

  • If the customer says they didn't get an email, ask them to look for Kerry O'Neill in their inbox or spam folder, not localmile.plus.
  • The customer's relationship for billing sits with MailPlus head office. The relationship for service sits with you. The two emails come from different places on purpose.

The email contains a single green button: VIEW & ACCEPT TERMS. When the customer clicks it, they land on a Service Commencement Form (SCF) hosted by MailPlus.

⌕ Click to enlarge
The customer's Service Commencement Form. They review the details you booked, tick a confirmation box, and accept terms.

The customer's three clicks

01
Open the email from MailPlus and tap VIEW & ACCEPT TERMS.
02
Review the details you booked, then tick “I have reviewed and confirmed my information.”
03
Click ACCEPT TERMS & CONDITIONS. The job is released to the franchisee.

What you see on the LPO side

While the customer is doing the above, the job sits in your Awaiting T&C view (and on the customer's card in Customer Hub) with a cream Awaiting T&C badge.

⌕ Click to enlarge
A customer waiting on T&C acceptance. They will not appear in your Active Today list until they accept.

You don't need to do anything during this wait. The status updates automatically the moment the customer accepts. Most customers accept within ten minutes; some take a day or two.

If they haven't accepted within an hour

The most common reason is the email landed in spam. The second most common is a typo in the email address you entered at Step 1. A quick phone call usually resolves both:

“Hi, just checking — did you get an email from Kerry O'Neill at MailPlus a little while ago? If you can find it and tap the green button, we can get your pickup locked in for tomorrow.”

What happens once they accept

The moment the customer ticks the box and clicks ACCEPT TERMS & CONDITIONS:

  • The customer's record in localmile.plus flips from Awaiting T&C to Active.
  • The job moves into the franchisee coordination phase — covered in Section 5.
  • You don't need to refresh anything; the change shows up next time you look at the screen.
04 / 10
Section 05

Franchisee coordination.

Once T&Cs are accepted (or skipped, if you're paying), the job lands with the franchisee for review. They confirm they can do it, raise any questions, and accept. This back-and-forth is what the Job Request Coordination page is for.

Who's in this chat — and who isn't

The Coordination Chat is between you (the LPO) and the franchisee driver, only. The customer does not see this chat and is not copied into the messages. It exists so you can sort out timing, access, and special instructions before the driver shows up.

What the page looks like at first

⌕ Click to enlarge
The empty Coordination page. Client details on the left; chat panel on the right. Above it all, a CANCEL REQUEST button if you need to pull the job back.

The left column shows everything the franchisee needs to know:

  • Client details — company, contact, phone, email.
  • Location — full address.
  • Service logistics — service type, billing arrangement, date, frequency.
  • Instructions — anything you put in the Special Instructions box at booking. This is where “collect from Level 3” or “ring buzzer 4” appears.

Starting the conversation

You don't have to send a message — if everything in the brief is clear, the franchisee will simply accept the job. But for a new customer, a quick context note often saves the franchisee a phone call. Useful first messages:

⌕ Click to enlarge
A typical opening message — context the brief alone doesn't capture. The franchisee replies inline.
📝 Useful things to add in the first message

“He especially liked that it's the same driver, and could provide the gate code. He has a young baby, hates it when couriers knock on the door and wake the baby — easy to resolve.”

Anything that's about the relationship rather than the job itself — preferences, sensitivities, who to ask for, parking quirks — belongs in the chat. The job brief covers the what, where, and when. The chat covers the how.

The back-and-forth

⌕ Click to enlarge
The franchisee replies. Once they're satisfied, they accept the job and the page closes itself out.

You'll get most replies within an hour or two during business hours. If you see no response after a full business day, that usually means the franchisee is on the road — they catch up on coordination at the end of the run. There is no urgency for you to chase; the job sits paused safely in the meantime.

What ends the coordination phase

The coordination ends one of two ways:

  • The franchisee accepts the job. The job moves into your active schedule, and the customer gets a “Booking confirmed” email with the start date. This is the normal happy path.
  • You cancel the request using the red CANCEL REQUEST button. Use this only if the customer has changed their mind, the address is wrong, or something fundamental has shifted. The customer is not automatically notified — you would need to ring them.
If the franchisee declines

Occasionally a franchisee will decline a job — usually because the address is outside their territory or the service type isn't something they cover. The job moves to your Declined view (see Section 6) and you'll need to either rebook with adjusted parameters or contact MailPlus to find a different operator.

05 / 10
Section 06

Running the day from Job Manager.

Booking a job is the front of the work. Watching jobs move through the day is the rest of it. The Job Manager is built so you can answer the only two questions you ever really need to ask: what's happening today, and what's waiting on someone else.

The six views in the Views panel

Down the middle of Job Manager is a Views panel that filters the list to whatever slice you care about right now. Six views, in the order you'll use them:

Pending Requests
Jobs that are still in the T&C or coordination phase — not yet locked in. The number on this view is the most useful single number on the screen: it's how many things need someone else's input before they happen. If this is zero, you're not blocking anything.
Active Today
Every job scheduled for the current day. This is your morning view. Open it first thing, scan the list, and you know what to expect at the counter and what's going out on a route.
Future One-Off
Booked one-off jobs that haven't happened yet. Useful when a customer rings to ask “is my Friday pickup still on?”
Recurring Schedules
All standing arrangements at a glance. Section 7 covers this view in detail — calendar, day selectors, skip-a-visit, and ending a series.
History
Completed jobs. Useful for invoicing questions and dispute resolution.
Declined
Jobs the franchisee couldn't take. These need a follow-up — either a rebook with different parameters or a phone call to MailPlus.
⌕ Click to enlarge
A job in the Pending Requests view, mid-coordination. Note the CHAT & MANAGE button — one click straight back into the conversation with the franchisee.

Reading a job card

Every row in Job Manager is a job card with the same anatomy:

  • Customer name — top left, bold.
  • Status badge — top right (see below).
  • Contact details — name, email, phone, suburb.
  • Service summary — Site to LPO, billing type.
  • Reference — the job ID, useful when a customer quotes it.
  • Action button — Chat & Manage for pending jobs, Contact Operator for active jobs.
Reading the status badges
Pending Scheduled In-Progress Completed Declined

Pending — coordination not yet finished. Waiting on the franchisee or the customer.

Scheduled — accepted, locked in, but not happening today.

In-Progress — the franchisee has accepted today's instance and is on route.

Completed — done. Lives in History from this point.

Declined — a franchisee couldn't take it. Needs follow-up.

A morning routine that takes ninety seconds

  1. Open Job Manager.
  2. Click Pending Requests. If the number is zero, move on. If not, glance at each — a customer waiting on T&C? A franchisee waiting on your reply? Action whatever's blocking.
  3. Click Active Today. Scan the list. Note who's coming and roughly when.
  4. Done. Carry on with your morning.

That's it. localmile.plus is built so the system tells you what needs your attention, rather than asking you to remember. If a job is on the page, it's on track. If it isn't, it isn't your problem yet.

06 / 10
Section 07

Recurring schedules.

Most of your business customers will have a regular pattern: every weekday, Monday-Wednesday-Friday, Tuesday-and-Thursday. Once it's set up, you don't need to rebook each visit. Recurring Schedules is the page where you can see the whole rhythm at once — and change it when life happens.

The calendar view

⌕ Click to enlarge
A month at a glance. Each pill is a scheduled visit; click any pill to open the customer's full schedule.

The calendar shows every recurring instance for the month — every customer, every day they're due. Hover over a pill to see the service type. Click a pill to open the Schedule Management modal for that customer. There's a List toggle in the top right if you prefer to see schedules as rows instead of as a calendar.

The Schedule Management modal

⌕ Click to enlarge
Schedule Management for one customer. The day buttons at the top set the standing pattern; the list below shows the next several upcoming visits, each with a Skip option.

The modal has three things you can do, in order from least drastic to most:

Skip a single visit
Tap Skip Visit next to a date in the upcoming instances list. The customer drops out of that day's schedule and is back to normal the next visit. Use this when the customer rings to say “we're closed Thursday.”
Change the standing days
Tap or untap the day buttons (Mon, Tue, Wed, Thu, Fri) at the top. The change applies to all future visits — past and current ones aren't affected. Use this when a customer's pattern changes permanently.
Stop all future visits
The red STOP ALL FUTURE VISITS button at the bottom. This ends the recurring arrangement entirely. The customer is not deleted — they remain in your Customer Hub — but no more visits will generate. Use this when a customer cancels their service.
Public holidays and weekends

Recurring schedules only run on business weekdays. Weekends are not selectable in the day picker. Public holidays for your state are excluded automatically — you don't need to skip them manually. If a customer's regular Monday lands on Australia Day, the visit simply doesn't generate, and the next Monday is the next visit.

If a customer wants a one-off in addition to their schedule

Don't add the day to their schedule and skip it later. Instead, use Customer Hub → + symbol on their card → BOOK NEW JOB to create a one-off pickup outside the recurring pattern. The two will run in parallel without interfering with each other.

07 / 10
Section 08

Your bookings@localmile.plus mailbox.

Most of what you need is in the localmile.plus app. But some things still arrive by email — customer replies, system confirmations, occasional notifications. The bookings@localmile.plus mailbox is where they land. This section is what to expect there, and what's worth opening versus what's just an audit trail.

What lands in the mailbox

What you'll see Why it arrived Does it need action?
Customer reply to a booking confirmation The customer hit reply on a system email — “please come earlier on Friday,” “skip next week.” Yes. Read it and action it in localmile.plus (skip a visit, update the schedule, or reply by email).
System notification — booking confirmed A copy of the “Booking confirmed” email sent to a customer. No. Logged for your records.
System notification — service today A copy of the morning-of email the customer received. No. Logged for your records.
T&C accepted confirmation A customer has accepted T&Cs and the job has moved to coordination. Usually no — the app shows the same status. But useful if a customer rings asking whether you got their acceptance.
Coordination chat notification A franchisee replied in a coordination chat. Yes — open localmile.plus and reply in the chat.

What's not in this mailbox

Three things are deliberately routed elsewhere:

  • T&C invitation emails to customers — these come from kerry.oneill@mailplus.com.au, not from bookings@localmile.plus. If a customer says they didn't get one, that's the address to ask them to look for.
  • Invoices — handled in NetSuite, not here.
  • Internal MailPlus communications — those go to your personal LPO email, not the bookings inbox.

How to keep it tidy

The mailbox grows quickly because every system email logs a copy. Two habits that keep it manageable:

  • Archive system notifications weekly. They're an audit trail, not an action list. Once you've glanced at them, they can leave the inbox.
  • Action customer replies the same day. Reply in localmile.plus where possible (the customer can email you back through the system reply path); only use email reply for things that genuinely need a written paper trail.
When to use which channel

In-app coordination chat — anything LPO ↔ franchisee. Timing, access, special instructions, route notes. Stays attached to the job.

Email reply (via bookings@localmile.plus) — anything LPO ↔ customer that needs a record. Service changes, pricing questions, dispute notes.

Phone — anything sensitive, urgent, or unclear. Then capture the outcome in one of the above.

08 / 10
Section 09

Cheat sheet.

Print this page. Stick it next to the counter. The whole pack distilled into eight cards you can scan in under a minute.

Morning routine

  1. Open Job Manager.
  2. Click Pending Requests. If zero, skip.
  3. Click Active Today. Glance at the list.
  4. Carry on.

Booking a new customer (3 steps)

  1. Site — company, contact, address, instructions.
  2. Service & schedule — billing, pickup type, date, recurring? days.
  3. Review — read it back, then REQUEST JOB.

Booking a returning customer

Customer Hub → find their card → +

Skips Step 1. Two screens and you're done.

What to say at the end of the call

“You'll get an email from MailPlus in the next couple of minutes asking you to confirm your details. Tap the green button at the bottom and tick the box. We're good to go for [day]. If you don't see it, check your spam folder.”

Status badges, at a glance

Awaiting T&C — customer hasn't accepted yet.

Pending — franchisee in coordination.

Scheduled — locked in, future date.

In-Progress — driver on route today.

Completed — done; lives in History.

Declined — needs follow-up.

Recurring schedules

Skip Visit — single absence.

Change days — tap day buttons.

Stop All Future Visits — ends the series.

Public holidays excluded automatically.

If T&Cs aren't accepted in an hour

Ring the customer:

“Just checking — did you get the email from Kerry O'Neill at MailPlus? Tap the green button and we're locked in.”

Most likely cause: spam folder.

When to use which channel

App chat — LPO ↔ franchisee.

Email reply — LPO ↔ customer.

Phone — anything urgent or unclear.

09 / 10
Section 10

Where to get help.

Most of the time, localmile.plus does its own work in the background. When you need a hand, the app already knows how to reach the right people — you don't have to remember email addresses or phone numbers. Two routes, both built in.

Route one — from inside any job

Every job card has a REF: hyperlink in the bottom right corner. Click it.

⌕ Click to enlarge
The reference link on a job card. This is the fastest path to support when the question is about a specific customer or pickup.

Clicking it opens a Job Inquiry box that's already tagged with the job reference. Type what's on your mind, hit SEND MESSAGE, and it goes straight to the MailPlus support team as an escalation notification — with the full context of the job already attached.

⌕ Click to enlarge
The Job Inquiry box. The reference is already filled in for you — you only need to write the question.
When to use this

Anything tied to a specific job — wrong franchisee, customer issue, billing query, schedule problem. The MailPlus team gets it with all the context attached, so you don't need to explain the backstory.

Route two — the Support Center

For everything that isn't tied to a specific job, there's a dedicated Support Center. You'll find it at the bottom of the left sidebar, next to the question-mark icon.

⌕ Click to enlarge
Sidebar expanded. The Support Center sits below My Profile and is reachable from any screen.
⌕ Click to enlarge
The Support Center. Kerry O'Neill is your named contact for everything localmile.plus, with email and phone right there. User Documentation and Direct Inquiries (for feature requests and platform feedback) sit alongside.

If you'd rather call

The numbers and emails surfaced inside the app are the same ones below — kept here for reference and for the rare moment the app itself is the problem.

Dedicated localmile.plus support
Kerry O'Neill
Your named contact for anything localmile.plus — T&C issues, account questions, day-to-day support. Available Mon–Fri, 9am–5pm AEST.
Email · kerry.oneill@mailplus.com.au
Phone · 0409 244 890
Onboarding specialist
Liam
For getting started, training questions, and “how do I do X in localmile.plus?” — your first weeks on the platform.
Reach via Kerry or the Support Center.
General MailPlus support
MailPlus Australia
For anything broader than localmile.plus — operational matters, escalations, things that span billing or dispatch.
Phone · 1300 65 65 95
Platform & technical
localmile.plus Development Team
Ankith
For things that look broken — buttons that don't work, statuses stuck, errors on screen. Worth a screenshot. Reach them via the Job Inquiry route or through Kerry.
Send via REF: link or Support Center.
Feature requests & feedback
Direct Inquiries
For ideas, feature suggestions, or platform feedback — anything that's about localmile.plus the product, not a specific issue.
Support Center · Send Feedback

The whiteboard test

One closing thought, looping back to where this pack started. The whole point of localmile.plus is to retire your whiteboard. If at any point you find yourself reaching for one — pen behind ear, sticky note in hand — that's a signal something in the app isn't working the way it should be. Tell us. The Support Center and the Job Inquiry route both go to the team that can fix it. We are genuinely keen to hear when it falls short.

Welcome to localmile.plus. We hope it earns its place at your counter.

10 / 10 — End of pack
